coredata.dc:title: Improved dielectric performance of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) - Carbon dots composites
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Aravinda, T.
Rao, Srilatha
Kumbla, Vidhyashree
Pattar, Jayadev
coredata.dc:description: Polymer nanocomposites with high dielectric constant and low-loss tangent materials have been extensively used in devices. Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF)-carbon dot (C-dot) nanocomposites have been synthesized to investigate the effect of C-dot on AC conductivity and dielectric properties. The FTIR spectra of synthesized samples confirm the formation of composites. The broadening of the peak in XRD patterns with an increase in the C-dot concentration has been observed, indicating the interaction of PVDF with C-dot. Morphological studies were carried out using Scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Frequency-dependent conductivity and dielectric properties were studied in the frequency range of 10 Hz to 8 MHz. AC conductivity graphs were found to obey Jonscher's power law and show no contact loss behavior. Prepared samples show improved dielectric constant with an increase in C-dot filler concentrations. Moreover, all samples show tangent loss of less than 0.015 at 1 kHz frequency. These results demonstrate that composite possesses broad application prospects in device electronics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PVDF- C dot
Dielectric properties
AC conductivity
Polymer composites
Interfacial polarization

Authors also acknowledge the support from REVA University for the facilities provided to carry out the research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of the pure PVDF and C-dot doped PVDF samples.
XRD patterns of PVDF and PVDF C-dot composite samples.
SEM images of 0.5%, 1% and 3 wt% C-dot PVDF composites.
AC conductivity of PVDF and nanocomposites inset variation at higher frequencies.
Jonscher's law fitting for 3 wt % of C-dot in PVDF nanocomposite.
Frequency-dependent dielectric constant of PVDF and PVDF/C-dot nanocomposites.
Variation of dielectric loss of PVDF and nanocomposites.
Dielectric constant, loss tangent of the composites as a function of wt. % of C-dot in PVDF at 1 kHz frequency.

head.ce:title: Improved dielectric performance of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) - Carbon dots composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nitte Meenakshi Institute of Technology, Bangalore, 560064, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Nitte Meenakshi Institute of Technology, Bangalore 560064, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, Alva's College, Moodubidire, 574227, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, School of Applied Sciences, REVA University, Yelahanka, Bangalore, 560064, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, School of Applied Sciences, REVA University, Yelahanka, Bangalore 560064, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymer nanocomposites with high dielectric constant and low-loss tangent materials have been extensively used in devices. Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF)-carbon dot (C-dot) nanocomposites have been synthesized to investigate the effect of C-dot on AC conductivity and dielectric properties. The FTIR spectra of synthesized samples confirm the formation of composites. The broadening of the peak in XRD patterns with an increase in the C-dot concentration has been observed, indicating the interaction of PVDF with C-dot. Morphological studies were carried out using Scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Frequency-dependent conductivity and dielectric properties were studied in the frequency range of 10 Hz to 8 MHz. AC conductivity graphs were found to obey Jonscher's power law and show no contact loss behavior. Prepared samples show improved dielectric constant with an increase in C-dot filler concentrations. Moreover, all samples show tangent loss of less than 0.015 at 1 kHz frequency. These results demonstrate that composite possesses broad application prospects in device electronics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, extensive attention has been given to polymer nanocomposite materials that use nanometer-scale fillers [1–3]. Due to their novel properties and applications, polymer composites are cutting-edge scientific and technical research materials. The ability to tune their mechanical, electrical and optical properties made them a potential candidate in sensor, laser, optoelectronics, medical devices, and other applications [4–6]. Among many polymers, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) is mostly used polymer because of its prominent traits like high dielectric permittivity, polycrystallinity, pyroelectricity/piezoelectricity, and chemical resistance [7–10].

In recent days, compounds of carbon which are graphite, carbon quantum dots (C-dots), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, and fullerene emerge as attractive materials in various applications. Their abundance, non-toxicity, and low cost with unique electrical, mechanical, and optical properties have made carbon compounds a highly important class of materials [11,12]. Amidst other carbon compounds, carbon dots (C-dot) stand out distinctly because of their long lifetime of emission, harmonious size with living cells and macromolecules, and stable photoluminescence. These traits make C-dots productive in the area of photocatalysis, optoelectronics, energy storage, bio-applications, and sensors [13–15]. The presence of conductive fillers in polymer matrix reveals distinctive dielectric properties to the composite which depends on the type, distribution, and size of reinforced materials. The addition of carbon dots to the polymer matrix may create a conductive path for the charge carriers. Therefore, in the present work, a small amount (0.5, 1, 3 wt %) of C-dots was used as a filler in PVDF to study the AC conductivity and dielectric properties. The synthesis and systematic analysis of the obtained results were discussed and presented in this paper.

One-pot synthesis of organic soluble C-dots was made as reported earlier by Zheng et al. [16]. The purchased cetylpyridinium chloride (CPC) was kept drying for 3 h In a vacuum to prepare a 15 mM stock solution in water. With constant stirring of 2 M, NaOH was added to it dropwise at ambient temperature. When NaOH is added, the color of the solution changes from colorless to pale yellow and then to wine red confirming the carbonation of CPC. The pH of the solution was maintained at 7 by adding HCl dropwise and C-dots were extracted using dichloromethane. To remove water-soluble compounds, the prepared solution was washed with water, brine, and CH2Cl2 and dried in a vacuum.

Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) with a molecular weight of 1,80,000 GPC is brought from Sigma-Aldrich Co, Germany. Dimethyl formamide (DMF) was purchased from Merck India, Ltd. And was utilized without any purification. Diffusion Induced Phase Separation (DIPS) technique was used for the preparation of PVDF membranes. The solution containing 2 g PVDF in 10 mL of DMF was prepared by mild stirring for 24 h at a constant temperature of 60 °C. The obtained viscous solution was cast over non–woven support (18 cm × 20 cm) connected by a casting Knife with a 250- μm gate open (The K-Paint Applicator RK Print Coat Instruments, Ltd., UK). Further, this Casted membrane was kept in an oven at 80–100 °C for about 2 min to evaporate the excess solvent. Finally, the membrane was separated by spraying water at the sides and stored in double-distilled water for 24 h. Carbon-Dot-PVDF membranes were prepared by similar methods with weight variants of carbon dots in the manner 0.5 wt %, 1 wt %, 3 wt %.

PerkinElmer (Spectrum RX1) FTIR spectrometer is used to obtain the FTIR profile of PVDF and PVDF/C-dot composites in the spectral range of 4000 cm−1 to 450 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. Structural patterns were recorded by using a Rigaku X-ray diffractometer (λ = 1.541 Å). Surface studies of PVDF and its composites were carried out using SIRION field emission Scanning electron microscopy (SEM). LCR meter, (HIOKI-IM3536) and a four-terminal probe (HIOKI-L2000) is used for the measurement of AC electrical characteristics and dielectric properties in the frequency range of 10 Hz to 8 MHz. All the measurements were carried out at room temperature.

FTIR spectra of the pure PVDF and C-dot doped PVDF samples are shown in Fig. 1 . The peaks at wavenumber 485, 600, 743, and 1036 cm−1 are the peaks of the α-crystal phase. The peaks at 509 and 842 cm−1 are the characteristic peaks of β-crystal shape [17]. The peaks at 882, 1036, 1400, 1420, 1666, 2979, and 3017 cm−1 show different phases of PVDF [18]. The composites of PVDF C-dot also show similar peaks indicating not many changes in the structures of PVDF and PVDF/C-dot composites. However, careful observations have shown a small peak shift from their peak values. The peak appeared at 600 cm−1 is shifted to 597 cm−1, 1420 cm−1 to 1402 cm−1 and 1666 to 1673 cm−1 in the case of composites. This type of peak shift in composites confirms the interaction of PVDF with C-dot.

An X-ray diffractogram of PVDF and PVDF/C-dot composites has been shown in Fig. 2 . It is observed that all samples have a prominent peak located at 2θ = 20.2 corresponding to the (110) plane [19]. This peak is appearing in all samples as a prominent peak indicating the creation of β-phase in the material. The peak at 2θ = 39.54 indicates a (021) plane. The other peaks at 18.2 represent the α-phase of the material. Since this peak was quite broad, it strongly overlapped with the remaining α-phase. The addition of C-dot into the polymer matrix enhances the intensity of peaks as well as the broadening of the peak. This indicates that there is an interaction between polymer and C-dot.

Fig. 3 a shows a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of the pure PVDF layer, which is rough and dense where there are no empty spaces but there are small fold lines on the surface. As C-dot is introduced in the PVDF matrix the surface seems to be smooth and black as indited in Fig. 3 a-d. The modification in the morphology is also observed by the change in the brightness of the samples due to the difference in densities [20]. In all composites, pores were observed visually compared to pristine PVDF which is due to the incorporation of C-dot in the polymer matrix. The sizes of the pores increase with the increase in C-dot content. The results would indicate the enhancement of membrane hydrophilic properties [21]. These observed outcomes were supported by FTIR results.

Fig. 4 shows the frequency-dependent (AC) conductivity for PVDF and its composites. At the low-frequency range, all samples show a low-value plateau indicating DC conductivity which is very small. At a higher frequency range, conductivity increases exponentially indicating a frequency dispersion. The variation of conductivity at a higher frequency is shown in the inset fig which clearly shows some increase in AC conductivity with an increase in the content of C-dot. The variation in conductivity obeys Jonscher's power law given by
(1)
σ
( ω )
= σ
( 0 )
+ A
ω S
where A is a temperature-dependent pre-exponential factor and s is frequency exponent (0 < s < 1). The term σ(0) is independent of frequency indicating dc conductivity. The second term represents short-range hopping or translational hopping [9,22]. The fitting curves are shown in Fig. 5 . The s parameter values being close to 0.9 indicates the No Contact Loss (NCL) type of AC conductivity for pure PVDF and the composites. When the C-dot is added to the polymer, the frequency-dependent AC conductivity with a comparable value to that of pure PVDF films is still maintained. Careful observation indicates that at 3 wt% C-dot addition there is a slight increase in the conductivity. Further increase in C-dot may increase the conductivity to many orders as observed would be an increase in the conductivity [23].

Recently, polymer composites made with inorganic nanomaterials having high dielectric constant and low dielectric loss are very much advantageous because of their distinctive properties, like flexibility, and easy processing. The frequency-dependent variation in the dielectric constant for the composite samples is shown in Fig. 6 . With an increase in c-dot content, the dielectric constant values increase from 48.19 for PVDF to 63.38 for 3 wt % of C-dot at 10 kHz frequency. A similar type of result also has been reported for the other composites [24–26]. The increase of the dielectric constant in composite samples is because of interfacial polarization effects like Maxwell–Wagner–Sillars (MWS) [27,28]. In the low frequency region, accumulation of charge carriers takes place in the interfaces PVDF matrix and C-dot. Thus, the dielectric constant of the composite samples systematically increases with an increase in the content of the C-dot over the entire frequency range. At higher frequencies above 1 MHz, due to the slow relaxation phenomena of the polymer matrix, the dielectric constant slightly decreases [29]. This decrease in the dielectric constant at a higher frequency region is because of a reduction in the MWS and space charge polarization which are pronounced in composites compared to PVDF.

Tangent loss is one of the important dielectric properties which determines energy dissipation due to the applied external field. The interfacial connections between the polymer matrix and C-dot can be studied using dielectric loss. Fig. 7 shows the frequency dependence of tangent loss (δ) of PVDF and PVDF-C-dot nanocomposites at room temperature. The value of δ decreases gradually with an increase in frequency at a lower frequency range and then increases at a higher frequency. The tangent loss values of all samples show very low values less than 0.02 in the frequency range of 1 kHz–100 kHz. In general, contributions of conductivity and interfacial polarization are predominant at low frequencies whereas the high-frequency process is prominently associated with dipolar relaxation. The peaks after 1 MHz in all samples is attributed to the glass transition relaxation of the PVDF polymer matrix and are denoted as a relaxation [30,31]. Also, for the enhancement of dielectric properties of composites, the dispersion, and the spatial distribution of fillers into the polymer matrix the crucial factor. Therefore, the low dielectric loss value compared to the dielectric constant of composites infers the application of these materials in advanced technological fields like embedded capacitors.

The dependence of the dielectric properties of the composites on the wt. % of C-dot fillers at room temperature and 1 kHz is given in Fig. 8 . It is observed that the dielectric constant values increased sharply with an increase in C-dot concentration. Specifically, the dielectric constant increased from 48 to 64 as the C-dot content increased from 0 to 3 wt %. Whereas, tangent loss of composites remained at a low level (<0.015), in the corresponding filling range which was ascribed to the homogeneous dispersion of C-dot particles in the PVDF matrix and the fewer defects at the PVDF and C-dot interfaces. For dielectric device applications, the low dielectric loss of composite is preferred because of the less heat production. Therefore, the synthesized PVDF/C-dot composites have shown potential device applications.

PVDF and PVDF/C-dot composites have been synthesized to studied frequency-dependent properties. The formation of composites has been confirmed by the FTIR spectra and XRD patterns. Composite samples have shown a slight shift in FTIR peak indicating the interaction of C-dot with PVDF. XRD patterns of composite samples have shown similar peaks as that of PVDF. The peak broadening and increased peak intensity in composites samples again confirm the formation of the composites. PVDF/C-dot samples have shown pores in the morphology compared to PVDF with a smooth morphology. AC conductivity studies showed a slight increase in conductivity with an increase in the C-dot concentration in the PVDF matrix. Dielectric constant values increased systematically with an increase in the filler concentration. The MWS type of interfacial polarization phenomenon has been observed. Loss tangent values have shown very low values less than 0.015 for all the samples. The obtained results of enhanced dielectric constant and very low loss tangent values suggest that these materials have potential applications in device physics.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to thank AICTE, New Delhi for the financial support to carry out this work, through file AICTE, file number 8–35/FDC/RPS/POLICY-1/2021–22. Principal, NMIT Bangalore.
Authors also acknowledge the support from REVA University for the facilities provided to carry out the research.
